title: Sourdough sandwich loaf
subtitle: Naturally leavened white loaf in a tin, same-day bake
excerpt: A 900 g sourdough loaf baked in a tin, with a soft, close crumb suitable for sandwiches and toast. Leavened entirely with a ripe levain, no commercial yeast. Four hours of bulk fermentation with stretch-and-fold sets the structure; the tin keeps the shape regular.
sourceNotes: Sourdough bread is the oldest documented form of leavened bread. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the bread chapter describes a starter-leavened loaf baked in a tin with a close, soft crumb suited for slicing, noting that the fermented starter gives a more complex flavour than commercial yeast. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the household bread chapter includes instructions for building and maintaining a flour-and-water starter, and for producing a tin loaf with a consistent crumb using natural leavening alone.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze in a bag. Slices toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping and placing in the tin, cover with clingfilm and cold-prove overnight in the fridge for 8–12 hours. Bake straight from the fridge, adding 5 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Spray the oven walls with water before loading, reduce to 210°C after 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique used during bulk fermentation to develop gluten without full kneading. Wet the hand, reach under the dough on one side, stretch it up as far as it will go without tearing, and fold it over the top. Rotate the bowl 90 degrees and repeat four times. Each set takes about 30 seconds. Multiple sets over the first two hours build structure progressively.

content.text: A sourdough tin loaf produces the close, even crumb that a free-form boule cannot: no large holes, sliceable from edge to edge, good for sandwiches. The leavening comes entirely from the starter, so timing depends on how active yours is. A ripe levain, built 4 hours before mixing, is the key. If your kitchen is cool or your starter is sluggish, extend the bulk fermentation rather than rushing it.

Build your levain 4 hours before you want to mix: combine 50 g of your active {{sourdough-starter}} with 50 g of the {{strong-bread-flour}} and 50 g of water. Cover and leave at room temperature. It is ready when it has roughly doubled, looks bubbly throughout, and a small spoonful dropped into water floats.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}} and 320 ml of the {{water}} in a large bowl. Mix until no dry flour remains — do not knead. Cover and leave for 30 minutes. This rest develops gluten passively before the starter goes in.

Pour the ripe {{sourdough-starter}} over the dough. Dissolve the {{sea-salt-fine}} in the remaining 20 ml of water and add that too. Work everything together with wet hands, squeezing the dough through your fingers until the starter is fully incorporated and the salt is dissolved. The dough will feel shaggy.
Cover and leave at room temperature. During the first 2 hours, perform 4 sets of

, one set every 30 minutes. After the 4th set, leave the dough undisturbed for the remaining 2 hours. It is ready when it has grown by 50–75%, feels airy and slightly domed, and jiggles when you shake the bowl.

Lightly oil the loaf tin. Tip the dough onto an unfloured surface and stretch it into a rough rectangle. Fold the short sides in to the centre, then roll the dough away from you into a tight log the same length as your tin. Lower it seam-side down into the tin.
Cover loosely and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until the dough has risen to about 1 cm above the rim of the tin. Preheat the oven to 230°C fan.

Make one confident cut along the length of the loaf with a sharp knife. Spray the oven walls with water, slide the tin in, and close the door immediately. After 10 minutes reduce to 210°C and continue for 28–30 minutes until the crust is deep brown and the internal temperature reads 96°C. Turn out and cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before cutting.
